Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Online Methods states the ER1 targeting sequence was from CNE1 with 'Genbank ID#: AAA65967.1'.

Source_Species.reason: CNE1 is a yeast gene and the paper describes using the calnexin transmembrane domain as an ER routing tag in yeast.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper names CNE1/calnexin and uses yeast genomic DNA, but does not explicitly spell out 'Saccharomyces cerevisiae' as the source of CNE1 in the ER1 sentence.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The paper says endogenous targeting sequences were amplified from W303α genomic DNA, but also cites CNE1 reference sequence AAA65967.1; the exact phrasing 'W303α genomic source for targeting sequence context' is not explicit.

Applied_Species.reason: The ER1 tag was applied in engineered yeast strains.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied host throughout is S. cerevisiae.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: All strains were constructed from haploid W303α.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state microscopy vectors were built in pAG416GPD-ccdB, indicating a GPD promoter; ER1 was fused to COR1.3 in these constructs.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results and Methods describe ER1 as a 28 amino acid transmembrane domain from calnexin/CNE1 used as an ER routing tag.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The tag is explicitly described as an endoplasmic reticulum routing tag and used to localize fusion proteins to the ER.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: ER1 was directly tested for its effect on morphine production specificity and titer.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Morphine is an alkaloid/opiate in the paper's context.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Morphine is discussed as an opioid pharmaceutical/analgesic.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Morphine was measured in the culture medium.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show COR1.3-ER1 increased morphine specificity and reduced flux to neomorphine relative to untagged COR1.3.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Confocal microscopy validated localization of GFP/COR1.3 fusions, and metabolite profiling quantified morphine/neomorphine.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are directly supported by the localization and metabolic data in Fig. 2b and text.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The localization experiment was performed in engineered yeast cultures fed thebaine.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Authors explicitly state they cannot rule out that some effect may be due to reduced COR1.3 activity upon ER localization.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: ER1 was fused directly to COR1.3 and also GFP.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The Results support ER1 as a spatial engineering tool to improve product specificity in yeast metabolic engineering.

Other_Important_Info.reason: This is a non-catalytic engineering element directly tested in Results for flux control/localization.

Source_Species.reason: Results state T6ODM is from P. somniferum (opium poppy).

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Methods state all strains derive from W303α.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Results state initial genes were flanked by unique yeast promoters/terminators in YAC, and added genomic copies used the constitutive GPD promoter.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The paper directly reports heterologous expression in engineered yeast grown with thebaine.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: T6ODM is explicitly described as a 2-oxoglutarate Fe2+-dependent dioxygenase.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper implies untagged pathway enzymes are cytoplasmic in the localization comparison, but does not directly demonstrate T6ODM localization.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: T6ODM functions in the morphine/opiate morphinan pathway, part of BIA biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: It participates in the thebaine-to-morphine route and also the simplified T6ODM+morB route to hydrocodone/oxycodone.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: T6ODM acts on fed thebaine at the entry to the engineered pathway.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper states T6ODM direct product is neopinone, which rearranges to codeinone; this supports the stated intermediate pool description.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results explicitly describe oxidative demethylation of thebaine by T6ODM in a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent reaction leading to neopinone/codeinone.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: T6ODM initiates formation of precursors for codeine, morphine, hydrocodone, oxycodone, and hydromorphone in the tested strains.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The cautious wording matches Results: T6ODM is important, and increased T6ODM with CODM improved flux, but it was not claimed as sole rate-limiting.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The paper directly links T6ODM-containing pathways to morphine, hydrocodone, oxycodone, and hydromorphone production.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: These molecules are described as opioid pharmaceuticals/analgesics.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Products were measured in culture medium.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: T6ODM enabled conversion of thebaine into downstream opioids, and copy-number changes influenced production.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by heterologous expression, copy-number titration, and HPLC-MS metabolite profiling.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each point is directly supported by Results sections on pathway construction, copy-number balancing, and T6ODM+morB strain.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The listed treatments match culture conditions in Results/Methods.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results explicitly describe branching to neopine/neomorphine due to access of T6ODM product to COR before rearrangement.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper presents T6ODM as a key entry enzyme for microbial opioid production requiring pathway balancing.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Requirement for 2-oxoglutarate and context-dependent product distribution are explicitly discussed.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Initial YAC genes used unique yeast promoters/terminators; integrated copies used GPD promoter; ER1-tagged COR1.3 was also expressed in these systems.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: COR is described as an NADPH-dependent aldoketo reductase.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: COR1.3 is part of the morphine/morphinan BIA pathway.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper directly shows COR1.3 acts in the codeinone-to-codeine step and undesired neopinone-to-neopine branch.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results state COR catalyzes reversible reduction of codeinone to codeine and in yeast likely reduces neopinone to neopine.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: COR1.3 directly affects precursor formation for morphine and byproduct neomorphine.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports COR as a key branch-control step rather than establishing it as sole rate-limiting.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: COR1.3 is directly tied to morphine and neomorphine production.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: They are opioid pharmaceuticals/analgesics in the paper's context.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by isoform selection rationale, copy-number titration, ER targeting, confocal microscopy, and metabolite profiling.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four listed findings are explicitly supported in Results.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These conditions are described for the assays involving COR1.3.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper clearly presents COR1.3 tuning/localization as valuable for controlling product specificity.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Authors explicitly note lack of prior literature for COR activity on neopinone and connect it to neopine formation.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Initial YAC genes used unique yeast promoters/terminators and integrated copies used GPD promoter.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The paper directly tested heterologous CODM expression during thebaine conversion in yeast.
